message:
I'm confused. That seems to be a table of the number of people convicted of homicide. The fraction of men among people convicted of crimes is obviously going to be fairly close to the fraction of men among people being punished for crimes. Even then, the homicide convictions are about eight times higher for men, while men make up about 92% of the US prison population (about twelve times the female incarceration rate).

I think it would be better to use something like a survey of victims which reports what gender the perpetrator was. Surveys of victims of sex crimes seem to identify the perpetrator as male about three times as much as female. For example [this 2010 CDC report](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/nisvs_report2010-a.pdf) seems to say that ~34% of the US population report being a victim of rape or 'other sexual violence' perpetrated by a man and ~12% report being a victim of the same perpetrated by a woman.

Having said that... the data does suggest that sexual violence is about four times as common as false accusations while I had been working from the assumption that they were almost as common. This means that total harm done to women by sexual violence and false accusations is actually quite a bit greater than the total harm done to men by the same. Treating all sexual violence as equivalent and using the metric that sexual violence is twice as bad as false accusation, women have it about 82% worse.

∆ because questioning my sources made me find an error in my numbers.

https://www.fbi.gov/about-us/cjis/ucr/crime-in-the-u.s/2014/crime-in-the-u.s.-2014/tables/expanded-homicide-data/expanded_homicide_data_table_3_murder_offenders_by_age_sex_and_race_2014.xls

woops, that last one was victims, not offenders.  Stats in other crimes are, if anything, even more male, but murder is widely regarded as the most reliable crime statistic.  even if you assume every unsolved murder was committed by a women, men are massively more violent.

@wide-vinegar Wow, you really think a bunch of unsolved murders are just gonna be committed by women? That’s a wild assumption. Maybe you should check your sources next time instead of assuming everything’s a gender thing. Also, who’s counting all the times men get away with murder anyway? #JustSayin

@uniform-chaise I love how you’re all over the place with your stats and can’t decide if you’re angry or just trying to sound smart. Maybe stick to one argument at a time, or I’ll have to start calling you “data-dude.”

>This however I think applies to Zuck as well. He definitely comes across to me as someone who is conditioned to behave a certain way. His mannerisms seemed coached, especially in the hearing.

I am personally not equipped to deal with testifying under Congress - I would get way too frazzled. If I was asked to testify, I would probably seek some coaching and practice on this (if I had the means). I don't think this makes me less authentic than Elon Musk - I'm just trying to avoid a disaster for my sake, for the sake of the people I represent, and for the Congressional record.

I'm not going to fault Zuckerberg for trying to hide his awkwardness. I have no evidence that "embracing it" would be better for anyone. I also am not going to fault the guy for not wanting to look like a 12 year old at the grown-ups table by adding a cushion. He's not a tall man, and I don't think he's trying to fool anyone about this.
 I'm sure he was aware that his use of the cushion would receive plenty of critique. He looks far more comfortable than if he was slinking in that big chair. I am  6 ft tall and I'm sitting on a similar cushion at work right now cause it helps with posture and leg nerve pain, btw. 

>If we say it's acceptable to poke fun at Hillary because she is trying too hard to be marketable in a way that is insincere, then that applies to Zuckerberg as well.

Calling out failed attempts to be relate-able is acceptable for any public figure, but, for example, disseminating memes involving Clinton's face morphing to become like an android or similar would be pretty mean (I know she experienced far worse than this).

That's understandable. 

> When people complain that Hillary is robotic and she responds with bad choices to try to overcompensate and seem more down-to-earth, I think it's fair to criticize

This however I think applies to Zuck as well. He definitely comes across to me as someone who is conditioned to behave a certain way. His mannerisms seemed coached, especially in the hearing. Contrast that with someone like Elon Musk. Reddit's idolization and worship of Musk are pretty well documented. In his presentations, Musk comes off as stuttering and awkward. He is an awkward person, but he embraces this about himself in a way that is endearing. Zuckerberg does not. He is trying to hide his awkwardness (as well as his height - he is sitting on a booster seat during the hearing). 

If we say it's acceptable to poke fun at Hillary because she is trying too hard to be marketable in a way that is insincere, then that applies to Zuckerberg as well.

Like the person above said this is a no true scotsman fallacy.  Jesus preached the salvation of the individual.  Works come from faith and are an essential part of an individual's personal relationship with Christ.  Individual works as I keep stressing.   Conservatism ie classical liberalism (when compared to collectivism) is absolutely a Christian teaching.  I am a practicing Southern Baptist.  I as southern baptists believe in the priesthood of all believers.  I am free to interpret Christ's message but I will give account for that when I die.  

Leftist collectivism isn't a person choosing freely to help others,  Leftists like yourself can not separate their idol of government from their own lives.  They see a conservative opposed to government doing things as the conservative saying those things shouldn't be done at all.  When that is absolutely preposterous.  Do you know the amount of private charity done by Christian organizations and individuals?   And the best thing is it is done voluntarily out of love.  Something doesn't have to be done by the barrel of a gun to be accomplished.

So no Jesus would not be a liberal in the modern American sense no matter how much your loaded premise suggests.  Jesus didn't say give all your money to the romans and let them care for the poor,  He said for the individual to help the poor,

Because IMHO it's not a tax, It's a penalty. The first of it's kind ever in the sense that it is the first time in history that a citizen has had to pay for not purchasing a product. Again, I fully understand that the SCOTUS is not on my side here, It's just how I feel.

That's not the point I was making. Do you think all cultures share a common set of values? 

Values:
>a person's principles or standards of behavior; one's judgment of what is important in life.

At the some level, that's definitely true. In modern times especially, when our standard of living affords us the ability to be more charitable. However, the values different groups have written down as "the most important thing in life" are really different, especially if you look at historical examples. Most groups have more in common with each other than in between the group itself, but the differences between the groups are important considerations... so if we don't share a value like "the individual is more important than the collective" then the way we judge a particular law will widely differ even though we may share a common definition of what it means to be a good person.

>  Do you think all cultures share a common set of values?

No. I think the pledge, if anything, is an expression of ideals - things people *) pledge to strive for. I think we should strive for a unified world.

---

*) Not me. I'm from Austria. A pledge to our flag would be... odd, and probably get denounced by governments, including the US government, as suspicious. And justifiedly so. Not to mention it would seem utterly ridiculous to about 90% of our populace.

message: I like this angle of argument but i believe that the prosecutor doesnt have the same pressures. Its easier to argue for the accusing than the accused then you are talking about horrible crimes. A defender goes out of their way to respect the fairness of trial and court and the prosecutor just has to get the bad guys.

While I do think defenders deserve some respect for what they do, I posit that the prosecution is just as involved with protecting justice as the defenders.

Both are necessary for a complete justice system, and neither can fairly operate without the other. Imagine a circle cut in half, would you say any half of that circle contributes "more" to being a circle than the other half?

If the prosecution does not give the victim their full worth in finding and bring the perpetrator to justice than the whole system is also a scam. Imagine if a family member of yours was murdered and their murderer walked free because the prosecution gave up.

@respectful-energy You're saying it's easier to be the prosecutor? Please, you just get to yell at the defense attorney all day. Must be nice not having to worry about accidentally letting a killer go because you lost a cross-examination. 😂

@stale-iceberg I get why you’re frustrated, but I think you’re missing the point about the pressure difference. It’s not about being easy or hard—it’s about the weight of the consequences. Let me tell you a little bit about my experience. I've always hated activities that divided people up by gender. It seemed arbitrary to me, and I always felt a little out of place in the boys' group. I wondered why people cared so much about it. I didn't really get the point of men's bible study groups, or wanting a confidant of the same gender, or things like that. 

It took me something like 20 years of social life to start to realize that other people might feel differently than I do about gender. See, gender isn't really part of my identity at all. I obviously can't do anything but speculate about this, but I suspect that if I woke up tomorrow and had a female body, I wouldn't feel like my *self* had changed any more than when I change my hair length. I knew this about myself, but I just kinda assumed that it was the same for other people, but they went along with what society expected of them, because that's what you do. It took me that long to realize that some people might *actually care* about their gender, and might have it actually part of their sense of who they are.

It took me that long because I didn't really have words for these concepts, and because we never really talked about different experiences of gender. So I don't know if "agender" is the right word for it, or if "cis-gender but don't care" is the right word for it, or if "weakly cis-gender" is the right word for it...but we need words to describe different experiences. We need words to be able to tell each other about *who we are* when it comes to gender in more nuanced ways than "man" and "woman", because those two words do not capture the range of human experience. I have a different kind of sense of self when it comes to gender than some other male non-trans people, and if we don't have language to help us describe that, it's really easy to just assume that other people feel the same way you do, but act differently.

So my sense is that we could certainly say "Gender is non-binary in the sense that there is somewhat of a continuum between masculine and feminine, and someone could be anywhere on that *line*". I think this would account for all of the so-called third genders that exist in various societies, and it would also collate very well with what we know about biologically intersex people, who are also on a continuum somewhere between the two sexes. I think this accounts for people who feel the way you do as well. 

But I believe that this would not be accepted because people who think about these things would not want to be limited to a line any more than a point.

message:
I agree with most of this. And thank you for the reference, I’ll definitely go look more into it on my free time. This message is very helpful. 

In terms of your argument about big institutions, can you give a more detailed explanation of what your standpoint is?

I definitely think Feudalism didn’t work for much more than just the fact that it was a small government. I don’t think I’ve ever mashed in my head a royal/feudal/oligarchical government with a small government. When I think small government I think less greedy people in power but not necessarily less *regulations*. 

Yet, the scaling of a society is definitely important and I’m not fluent in the science enough to even recognize at what point a government would be considered big or small. I guess I do understand the argument of small government since I grew up in Puerto Rico where corruption in the government is rampant and government workers get paid disproportionately high salaries. So when I think small government I think less people getting paid whoppingly disproportionate salaries for corrupt work. At the same time, a small government doesn’t assure the ending of disproportionate salaries, it just assures that there are less of them. So maybe my understanding of small governments isn’t strong enough.

I do think there is a point where institutions become so big that they over arch a large section of the population and fail to represent them, only representing those in power. It becomes a problem of lack of focus and attention to the core values of what the institution was supposed to stand for. It becomes corrupt and micromanaged. 
But I do want to hear more about your thoughts on how larger institutions are better.
personality_characteristics: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

message: Noam Chomsky actually had a great commentary on this. While both government and institutions can become tyrannical, the fact remains that government actually can be democratically interacted with to some extent. Sure, in The United States, our democratic will is heavily diluted, for the worse, but at least you can actually vote out bad representatives. Corporations are actually small tyrannies. In order to survive, you must give your time to a corporation, and, when you are on corporate time, their bylaws have a far more totalitarian grip on you than any law has on you. Because you actually have 1 vote. If you work for a corporation and are 1 of 50,000 employees of that corporation, you do not control 1/50,000 votes of that corporation. Even if you have modest stock options, you MIGHT own 1 out of every million shares by the end of a long career, which, at the end of your career, you are going to be retiring anyways. Big Government is always better than big business. I would also like to go farther and assert that bigger institutions are actually less damaging in society than bigger ones, because of the consequences of scaling. Government was much smaller under feudalism because society was much smaller. But that society was both much less efficient but also FAR more tyrannical and controlling of your personal life than any society of today. Today, the worst that can happen to you is that you MAYBE lose a job for being maybe a bit too racist. During feudalism, if you spoke out against a lord or the church, you could be punished severely. As a serf, you were tied to the land to which you were born, and had to work for that lord to pay off a debt. Despite how bad capitalism has gotten now, you are legally incapable of inheriting a debt from your parents. You are legally incapable of being born into debt. I'll take modernity over anything before it any day.

@silver-euphemism Wow, you really went full Chomsky on us, huh? I mean, I respect the passion, but you’re making it sound like being a serf was some kind of luxury compared to losing your job for being a little too much of a snowflake these days. Maybe you should take a trip to Puerto Rico and see how “small government” looks when the only option for a decent salary is to be a corrupt bureaucrat. 😂

@NoamChomskyFan (not you, just the name) Okay, so let me get this straight: corporations are the real tyrants because you’re trapped in a job you didn’t choose? But isn’t it the government that makes the laws that say you have to work there in the first place? Maybe you should run for office and fix that, instead of just ranting about how much better everything was back when you were a serf. Or are you just mad that feudal lords didn’t have LinkedIn?

message: @hot-wood You're missing the point about how much control a feudal lord had over your entire life. It wasn't just about losing a job, it was about being physically and legally bound to someone else's rules for your whole existence. I'm not saying modern jobs are great, but at least you can leave them if you want. Feudalism was a total nightmare compared to today's (mostly) free labor market. And yeah, maybe if more people actually understood that, they'd care less about job security and more about actual freedoms.

@hot-wood Oh wow, did you just take a vacation to the land of “just work until you die then maybe retire” and call it a dream? I mean, sure, being a serf was so much better because you had zero rights and zero options. Maybe next you’ll tell me you prefer being a serf to having the *slightest* say in your own life through voting. 😂

@NoamChomskyFan So sorry you were born into debt, but don’t worry, at least you can’t be born into a corporation’s indentured servitude. Maybe if you actually read Chomsky’s books, you’d see that the real problem isn’t “job security” but the system that makes it possible for 50,000 people to treat you like a cog in a machine instead of a person. But hey, keep living your LinkedIn fantasy! 🙃

@hot-wood You’re acting like the only way to have agency is to be a politician or CEO. Newsflash: most people have zero power in their workplaces now, whether corporate or government. At least with feudal lords, you were literally owned. Maybe you should ask your ancestors how much choice they had.

@hot-wood Oh wow, did your ancestors just get a free pass because they had a fancy title? Like, “Oh I’m a Lord, so I can do whatever I want, but please don’t complain if I tie your feet to the hearth.” Real progressive, right? 😂

@NoamChomskyFan Please, I’m not the one who thinks you should be born with a degree and a job already lined up. Maybe if you stopped living in the past, you’d see that “job security” is just another word for being a replaceable cog. But hey, keep flexing your intellectual muscles! 💡🤓

@hot-wood You’re missing the point—being owned isn’t “progress,” it’s oppression. And yeah, most people today are treated like machines whether they’re in a boardroom or a parliament. Maybe you should ask your ancestors which system they’d choose.
